and commonly known as **Birmingham 2022**, were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") for members of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") that took place in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), England between 28 July and 8 August 2022.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) They were the 22nd edition of the Commonwealth Games. It was the third and seventh time England and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") hosted the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), respectively.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The **2022 Commonwealth Games**, officially known as the **XXII Commonwealth Games** and commonly known as **Birmingham 2022**, were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") for members of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") that took place in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), England between 28 July and 8 August 2022.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) They were the 22nd edition of the Commonwealth Games. It was the third and seventh time England and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") hosted the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), respectively.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

4,822 athletes, including 311 para athletes from 72 Commonwealth Games Associations took part in the event. The event took place at 15 venues across Birmingham, [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)") and [London](/wiki/London "London") and featured 19 [Commonwealth sports](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_sports "Commonwealth Games sports").[[4]](#cite_note-4) It was also the first major multi-sport event to have more events for women than men and had the largest integrated para-sport programme in the history of the Commonwealth Games with 8 para sports.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Australia topped the medal table by both total golds (67) and total medals (179), with host nation England finishing second with 58 golds and 176 total medals and Canada finishing third with 26 golds and 92 total medals. India finished fourth with 22 gold and 61 total medals, and New Zealand finished fifth with 20 gold and 49 total medals.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Niue won its first ever Commonwealth Games medal.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Birmingham was announced as the host city on 21 December 2017.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The event marked the last time that the Commonwealth Games were held under [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") prior to her [death on 8 September of that year](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Elizabeth_II "Death and state funeral of Elizabeth II"), which was exactly one month after the conclusion of the Games.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The event contributed £1.2 billion to the economy of the United Kingdom.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Host selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Host selection")]

Main article: [Bids for the 2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Bids_for_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games "Bids for the 2022 Commonwealth Games")

Two cities initially launched bids for the games: [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban"), South Africa, and [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), Canada. Edmonton withdrew its bid in February 2015, leaving Durban as the only bid to go forward to [CGF](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") General Assembly in September 2015.[[12]](#cite_note-euronews.com-12) [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban") initially secured the right to host the games, as they were the sole bidder for the event. The city previously considered bidding for the [2020](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") or [2024 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2024_Summer_Olympics "2024 Summer Olympics"), but later dropped the idea as it wanted to focus on the 2022 Commonwealth Games.[[13]](#cite_note-13) It would have marked the first time the games were held in Africa and the second time a [Commonwealth republic](/wiki/Republics_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations "Republics in the Commonwealth of Nations") would have hosted, following [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), India, in [2010](/wiki/2010_Commonwealth_Games "2010 Commonwealth Games"). The games were set to open on 18 July 2022, coinciding with the birthday of the late South African president, [Nelson Mandela](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela "Nelson Mandela").[[14]](#cite_note-14) It was reported in February 2017 that [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban") might be unable to host the games due to financial constraints.[[15]](#cite_note-15) This was confirmed one month later on 13 March 2017 when the CGF stripped Durban of their rights to host the Games.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The bidding process for the 2022 Commonwealth Games was relaunched in March 2017 where English cities [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") expressed their interests in hosting the Games.[[17]](#cite_note-Liverpool-Birmingham-17) On 14 March 2017, [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") also expressed an interest in hosting the Games.[[18]](#cite_note-Manchester-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Birmingham_2022_Flag.jpeg/250px-Birmingham_2022_Flag.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Birmingham_2022_Flag.jpeg)

Birmingham 2022 flag (with bidding logo) in [Victoria Square, Birmingham](/wiki/Victoria_Square,_Birmingham "Victoria Square, Birmingham"), in January 2018

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Library_of_Birmingham_celebrates_winning_the_Commonwealth_Games_2022_%2824354176257%29.jpg/250px-Library_of_Birmingham_celebrates_winning_the_Commonwealth_Games_2022_%2824354176257%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Library_of_Birmingham_celebrates_winning_the_Commonwealth_Games_2022_(24354176257).jpg)

[The Library of Birmingham](/wiki/Library_of_Birmingham "Library of Birmingham") was lit into different colours to celebrate the winning of the 2022 Commonwealth Games

By April 2017, the [British Government](/wiki/British_Government "British Government") asked city councils to submit proposals for hosting the 2022 Games.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Only Birmingham and Liverpool submitted official applications to the [Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Department_for_Digital,_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport").[[20]](#cite_note-20) London declined to bid, as it was focusing its efforts on preparing for the [World Athletics](/wiki/2017_World_Athletics_Championships "2017 World Athletics Championships") and [Para Athletics Championships](/wiki/2017_World_Para_Athletics_Championships "2017 World Para Athletics Championships").[[21]](#cite_note-21) On 27 April 2017, Manchester City Council announced that Manchester would not bid for the Games but could co-host the Games with other English cities.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In early September 2017, Birmingham was selected over Liverpool for the recommended bid for England.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The CGF announced that the deadline for the submission of bids was 30 September 2017 and its executive board was expected to announce the host by the end of 2017. [Commonwealth Games England](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_England "Commonwealth Games England") submitted the Birmingham bid before the deadline to the CGF to host the 2022 Games.[[25]](#cite_note-25) However, it was announced that the bid was not fully compliant, and the bidding process was extended until 30 November 2017.[[26]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-26) The CGF had 170 questions regarding Birmingham's bid.[[27]](#cite_note-27) On 21 December 2017, Birmingham was awarded for the 2022 Games as Durban's replacement host.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Louise Martin, president of the CGF, made the official announcement at a press conference at the Arena Academy in Birmingham.[[28]](#cite_note-:1-28)

## Development and preparation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Development and preparation")]

The [Birmingham Organising Committee for the 2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Birmingham_Organising_Committee_for_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games "Birmingham Organising Committee for the 2022 Commonwealth Games") (BOCCG) was responsible for the planning and operational delivery of the Games.[[29]](#cite_note-29) This includes sport, venue and competition management, ticket sales, all ceremonies and the Queen's Baton Relay. The headquarters of the organising committee is located in [One Brindleyplace](/wiki/Brindleyplace#One_Brindleyplace "Brindleyplace") building and has taken up the office until December 2022.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In March 2018, the BOCCG paid £25 million ($35 million) fee to the CGF for the right to host the 2022 Commonwealth Games. The fee included the £20 million ($28 million) for the Games hosting charges and £5 million ($7 million) for the development work in the Commonwealth.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In July 2018, British Prime Minister [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") appointed [John Crabtree](/wiki/John_Crabtree_(businessman) "John Crabtree (businessman)") as Chair of the BOCCG.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In January 2019, Ian Reid was announced as chief executive officer of the BOCCG.[[33]](#cite_note-33) On 6 June 2019, the [British Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") introduced the Commonwealth Games Bill which ensured the prohibition of unauthorised sales of Games tickets, effective flow of transport around Games venues, complete protection of commercial rights, and compliance with financial propriety rules by the government's funding of the BOCCG.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The bill received royal assent and was passed into law as the Birmingham Commonwealth Games Act 2020 on 25 June 2020.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In June 2020, it was announced that the entire schedule of the Games would be offset by one day to reduce conflicts with sporting events rescheduled due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), particularly the [UEFA Euro 2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020") (held on 11 June to 11 July 2021), the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") (held on 23 July to 8 August 2021), the [UEFA Women's Euro 2022](/wiki/UEFA_Women%27s_Euro_2022 "UEFA Women's Euro 2022") (held 6 to 31 July in England; the opening ceremony time would have conflicted with one of the semifinal matches), and the final days of the [2022 World Athletics Championships](/wiki/2022_World_Athletics_Championships "2022 World Athletics Championships") (held from 15 to 24 July).[[36]](#cite_note-36) All athletes and officials are required to test negative for COVID-19 on a [PCR test](/wiki/PCR_test "PCR test") before departure and upon arrival; infections during the Games will be handled on a case-by-case basis, with athletes not necessarily required to withdraw or publicly disclose their infection. [Commonwealth Games Australia](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Australia "Commonwealth Games Australia") considered the protocols to be less stringent and more "relaxed" than expected, and stated that it planned to impose stricter biosecurity protocols on its athletes to ensure their safety.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The 2022 Commonwealth Games took place in 15 venues spread across the [West Midlands region](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)"), with seven of them, including the athletics stadium and marathon route, located in the city of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"). The [National Exhibition Centre](/wiki/National_Exhibition_Centre "National Exhibition Centre") complex, located on the fringes of the city in neighbouring [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull"), hosted events in its exhibition halls and at the [Resorts World Arena](/wiki/Resorts_World_Arena "Resorts World Arena"). The six other regional hosts were [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), [Cannock Chase](/wiki/Cannock_Chase "Cannock Chase"), [Royal Leamington Spa](/wiki/Royal_Leamington_Spa "Royal Leamington Spa"), [Sandwell](/wiki/Sandwell "Sandwell"), [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick") and [Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton "Wolverhampton"). A sixteenth venue, the [Lee Valley VeloPark](/wiki/Lee_Valley_VeloPark "Lee Valley VeloPark") in Stratford, East London, hosted the track cycling.[[40]](#cite_note-Venues-40)

### Athletes' village

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Athletes' village")]

[Perry Barr](/wiki/Perry_Barr "Perry Barr") was selected to be the site of the athlete's village, with plans made to demolish the former [Birmingham City University](/wiki/Birmingham_City_University "Birmingham City University") campus after the university had moved to a new campus.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) The new village was supposed to house up to 6,500 athletes and officials during the games and then become 1,400 new homes after the games, as part of a wider regeneration effort.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Because of the COVID-19 pandemic, construction of the village fell significantly behind schedule[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) but the games' dates were not pushed back to accommodate this.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Because the village would not be ready in time for the games, on 11 August 2020 the BOCCG announced that the athletes and team officials will be housed in three 'campus' villages close to competition venues at the [University of Birmingham](/wiki/University_of_Birmingham "University of Birmingham"), the [University of Warwick](/wiki/University_of_Warwick "University of Warwick"), and the [NEC](/wiki/National_Exhibition_Centre "National Exhibition Centre"). Around 1,600 athletes and officials were set to be accommodated at the NEC campus, 1,900 at the University of Warwick campus, and 2,800 at the University of Birmingham campus.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In 2023, a year after the games had taken place and the first of the village's housing had been finished, the site was still uninhabited and described as a 'ghost village'.[[48]](#cite_note-48) In 2024, two years after the games, the council announced that it was going to sell a majority of the homes to a private developer - with a total loss of £320m expected.[[49]](#cite_note-49) The large losses from the village will compound on the [council's existing financial troubles](/wiki/Section_114_notice#Birmingham_City_Council,_2023 "Section 114 notice").

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Transport")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

The A34 flyover in Perry Barr was demolished in favour of a dual carriageway at ground level, cycle path and improved public transport services which were approved by [Birmingham City Council](/wiki/Birmingham_City_Council "Birmingham City Council") in October 2019 under its £27.1 million scheme.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

The city council announced in January 2020 that the existing National Express Bus Depot in Perry Barr would be demolished as the depot site would be used to construct the phase two of the athletes' village.[[51]](#cite_note-51) A replacement depot will be constructed on a largely council-owned land on nearby Aston Lane,[[52]](#cite_note-52) at a cost of £16 million, eight times the original estimate.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Perry_Barr_station_-_2022-10-_26_-_Andy_Mabbett_-_01.jpg/250px-Perry_Barr_station_-_2022-10-_26_-_Andy_Mabbett_-_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Perry_Barr_station_-_2022-10-_26_-_Andy_Mabbett_-_01.jpg)

Perry Barr railway station after rebuilding.

The [University railway station](/wiki/University_railway_station_(England) "University railway station (England)"), which serves the [University of Birmingham](/wiki/University_of_Birmingham "University of Birmingham"), was planned to be renovated and was expected to be complete in time for the Games. The station would make way for the University of Birmingham, which was due to host hockey and squash events for the Games.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) The new station was not finished in time for the event.[[57]](#cite_note-57) The [Perry Barr railway station](/wiki/Perry_Barr_railway_station "Perry Barr railway station") building was rebuilt and the platforms renovated as part of the £30.9 million project in [Perry Barr](/wiki/Perry_Barr "Perry Barr") and opened on May 29, 2022.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

[Transport for West Midlands](/wiki/Transport_for_West_Midlands "Transport for West Midlands") (TfWM) was scheduled to provide the city's first continuous cross-city bus route in time for the Games. The new Sprint bus route was to run an express service along the [A34](/wiki/A34_road "A34 road") and [A45](/wiki/A45_road "A45 road") between [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall") and [Birmingham Airport](/wiki/Birmingham_Airport "Birmingham Airport") and [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull") to Walsall via the [city centre](/wiki/Birmingham_city_centre "Birmingham city centre").[[59]](#cite_note-59) The service will be zero-emission with priority signals and extended bus lanes, along with "a swift boarding experience" to improve journey times and reliability.[[60]](#cite_note-60) In February 2020 it was announced that the [West Midlands Combined Authority](/wiki/West_Midlands_Combined_Authority "West Midlands Combined Authority") Board was planning to approve the £88 million funding and delivery schedule for Sprint in advance of the Games, to offer commuters and the Games visitors services to key venues including [Alexander Stadium](/wiki/Alexander_Stadium "Alexander Stadium"), [Arena Birmingham](/wiki/Arena_Birmingham "Arena Birmingham") and the [Resorts World Arena](/wiki/Resorts_World_Arena "Resorts World Arena").[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) As of November 2023, the service has yet to commence running.

### Cost and financing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cost and financing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Birmingham_2022_Countdown_Clock.jpg/250px-Birmingham_2022_Countdown_Clock.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birmingham_2022_Countdown_Clock.jpg)

The official countdown clock for the Games in [Centenary Square](/wiki/Centenary_Square "Centenary Square").

At the time of submission of the bid to the CGF, the bid committee announced that the event would cost £750 million.[[63]](#cite_note-63) On 25 June 2019, the [British Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") announced that the event costed £778 million. The British Government covered 75% (£594 million) and Birmingham City Council covered the remaining 25% (£184 million).[[64]](#cite_note-64) The budget is lower than the £967 million spent on the [Gold Coast 2018](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games"), but higher than the £543 million spent on the [Glasgow 2014](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games "2014 Commonwealth Games"). It is set to be the most expensive sporting event in the UK since the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") in [London](/wiki/London "London") which cost £8.8 billion.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The real cost will be published after completion.

### Ticketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ticketing")]

Over 1.3 million tickets were sold for the 2022 Commonwealth Games, which made the event the most attended Commonwealth Games ever to take place in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[66]](#cite_note-66) A ticket ballot for local residents opened on 14 July 2021, with the main public ticket ballot running from 8 to 30 September 2021.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

### Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Security")]

The [West Midlands Police](/wiki/West_Midlands_Police "West Midlands Police") said around 3,000 officers would be deployed to patrol the 2022 Commonwealth Games. 1,000 of these were to come from West Midlands Police and 2000 from a "mutual aid" arrangement with other UK forces.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Additionally, private drones were banned over a no-fly zone and 55 [police dogs](/wiki/Police_dog "Police dog") were also part of the security plan.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Festival")]

The 2022 Commonwealth Games was accompanied by a six-month long cultural event called the Birmingham 2022 Festival. It had been billed as "the biggest celebration of creativity ever seen in the region" and was taking place in the host city and throughout the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)"). With more than 200 events planned, it was one of the biggest cultural programmes ever hosted during the Commonwealth Games. Events took place before and after the 2022 Games, which took place between July 28 and August 8, 2022, from March to September 2022.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Queen's baton relay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Queen's baton relay")]

Main article: [2022 Commonwealth Games Queen's Baton Relay](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games_Queen%27s_Baton_Relay "2022 Commonwealth Games Queen's Baton Relay")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Cindy_Kiro_and_Richard_Davies_with_Queens_baton.jpg/250px-Cindy_Kiro_and_Richard_Davies_with_Queens_baton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cindy_Kiro_and_Richard_Davies_with_Queens_baton.jpg)

The baton held by Governor-General of New Zealand [Cindy Kiro](/wiki/Cindy_Kiro "Cindy Kiro") and viceregal consort [Richard Davies](/wiki/Richard_Davies_(doctor) "Richard Davies (doctor)").

The [Queen's baton](/wiki/Queen%27s_Baton_Relay "Queen's Baton Relay") of the 2022 Commonwealth Games was unveiled 29 September 2021. Designed as a collaboration between Birmingham Open Media, Raymont-Osman Product Design, Maokwo and Kajul,[[72]](#cite_note-72) it is constructed using [lost-wax casting](/wiki/Lost-wax_casting "Lost-wax casting"), 3D Printing, copper plating and CNC machining.[[73]](#cite_note-73) It incorporates [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium"), and [brass](/wiki/Brass "Brass") metals symbolising medals, and a strip of [platinum](/wiki/Platinum "Platinum") in observance of the [Queen's Platinum Jubilee](/wiki/Platinum_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Platinum Jubilee of Elizabeth II"). It also contains a [360-degree camera](/wiki/360-degree_camera "360-degree camera"), an LED lighting system tied to a [heart rate monitor](/wiki/Heart_rate_monitor "Heart rate monitor") (and displaying different effects when held by two people), and sensors to record environmental conditions—whose data will be analysed by a team at the [University of Birmingham](/wiki/University_of_Birmingham "University of Birmingham") following the relay.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-:6-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Laura Nyahuye, who led the team of five artists at Maokwo, stated that the baton was designed to "relate" to the countries and residents of the Commonwealth, and reflect the "authenticity and honesty" of its journey; these goals were met by avoiding the use of [precious metals](/wiki/Precious_metals "Precious metals"), and through its use of copper—which is designed to oxidise and develop a teal [patina](/wiki/Patina "Patina") over the course of the relay.[[75]](#cite_note-:6-75)

The relay began on 7 October 2021 at [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") in London, and lasted for 294 days—travelling through 72 Commonwealth nations and territories. After placing her message inside the baton, [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") presented it to British [Paralympic athlete](/wiki/Para-athletics "Para-athletics") and [cyclist](/wiki/Para-cycling "Para-cycling") [Kadeena Cox](/wiki/Kadeena_Cox "Kadeena Cox"). During the opening ceremony, the baton was delivered to [Charles, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles,_Prince_of_Wales "Charles, Prince of Wales"), who read the Queen's message to officially open the Games.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Birmingham_2022_Opening_Ceremony.jpg/250px-Birmingham_2022_Opening_Ceremony.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birmingham_2022_Opening_Ceremony.jpg)

Opening Ceremony of the Birmingham 2022 Commonwealth Games.

### Opening ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Opening ceremony")]

Main articles: [2022 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2022 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony") and [2022 Commonwealth Games Parade of Nations](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games_Parade_of_Nations "2022 Commonwealth Games Parade of Nations")

The opening ceremony of the 2022 Commonwealth Games were held at Alexander Stadium on the evening of 28 July 2022. Director [Iqbal Khan](/wiki/Iqbal_Khan_(director) "Iqbal Khan (director)") stated that the ceremony would aim to showcase the "vivid and vibrant confidence" of Birmingham. It was headlined by Birmingham-based [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music") band [Duran Duran](/wiki/Duran_Duran "Duran Duran"), while [Tony Iommi](/wiki/Tony_Iommi "Tony Iommi") of [Black Sabbath](/wiki/Black_Sabbath "Black Sabbath")–who is also a Birmingham native–performed alongside [Soweto Kinch](/wiki/Soweto_Kinch "Soweto Kinch") during a segment of the ceremony.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

### Closing ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Closing ceremony")]

The closing ceremony took place on 8 August 2022 at Alexander Stadium, and included the formal handover to the Australian state of [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)"), then host of the [2026 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2026_Commonwealth_Games "2026 Commonwealth Games") prior to its withdrawal nearly a year later before being replaced by Glasgow as the replacement host city, in a segment headlined by [Vanessa Amorosi](/wiki/Vanessa_Amorosi "Vanessa Amorosi").[[80]](#cite_note-80) The ceremony featured tributes to the industrial history of Birmingham, the [Windrush generation](/wiki/Windrush_generation "Windrush generation") and Birmingham TV show *[Peaky Blinders](/wiki/Peaky_Blinders_(TV_series) "Peaky Blinders (TV series)")*, and included performances from a number of famous West Midlands musicians and groups, including [Dexys Midnight Runners](/wiki/Dexys_Midnight_Runners "Dexys Midnight Runners"), [UB40](/wiki/UB40 "UB40"), [Panjabi MC](/wiki/Panjabi_MC "Panjabi MC"), [Musical Youth](/wiki/Musical_Youth "Musical Youth"), [Goldie](/wiki/Goldie "Goldie"), [Beverley Knight](/wiki/Beverley_Knight "Beverley Knight"), [Apache Indian](/wiki/Apache_Indian_(musician) "Apache Indian (musician)"), [Jorja Smith](/wiki/Jorja_Smith "Jorja Smith"), [the Selecter](/wiki/The_Selecter "The Selecter"), and [Laura Mvula](/wiki/Laura_Mvula "Laura Mvula"). The grand finale featured a surprise appearance by [Ozzy Osbourne](/wiki/Ozzy_Osbourne "Ozzy Osbourne") in his first live performance in three years, joined by [Tony Iommi](/wiki/Tony_Iommi "Tony Iommi") and former touring members of [Black Sabbath](/wiki/Black_Sabbath "Black Sabbath") [Tommy Clufetos](/wiki/Tommy_Clufetos "Tommy Clufetos") and [Adam Wakeman](/wiki/Adam_Wakeman "Adam Wakeman") for a medley of "[Iron Man](/wiki/Iron_Man_(song) "Iron Man (song)")" and "[Paranoid](/wiki/Paranoid_(Black_Sabbath_song) "Paranoid (Black Sabbath song)")".[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sports")]

See also: [Commonwealth Games sports](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_sports "Commonwealth Games sports")

### Commonwealth Games Charter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Commonwealth Games Charter")]

A new edition of the Commonwealth Games Charter came into effect for these Games. In addition to the ten core sports that were part of [Gold Coast 2018](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games") – [athletics](/wiki/Sport_of_athletics "Sport of athletics"), [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), [lawn bowls](/wiki/Lawn_bowls "Lawn bowls"), [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball") (for women), [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens"), [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"), [swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)") and [weightlifting](/wiki/Olympic_weightlifting "Olympic weightlifting") – five new sports will be integrated into the core sports: [road cycling](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing"), [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") (previously optional), [triathlon](/wiki/Triathlon "Triathlon"), [table tennis](/wiki/Table_tennis "Table tennis"), and [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"). The charter also mandates that a number of [parasports](/wiki/Parasports "Parasports") events (i.e. sports for elite athletes with disabilities) must be integrated within four core sports: athletics, lawn bowls, swimming and weightlifting (the lattermost is actually represented by a variation of powerlifting).

The charter also establishes the list of optional sports and disciplines that can be chosen by the organisation of each edition: [archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery") (recurve), [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") (3x3 or 5x5), [beach volleyball](/wiki/Beach_volleyball "Beach volleyball"), cycling ([mountain bike](/wiki/Mountain_biking "Mountain biking") and [track](/wiki/Track_cycling "Track cycling")), [rhythmic gymnastics](/wiki/Rhythmic_gymnastics "Rhythmic gymnastics"), and shooting (clay target, full bore, small bore and pistol). This same rule also establishes that the following events for athletes with disabilities are optional: [wheelchair basketball](/wiki/Wheelchair_basketball "Wheelchair basketball") (3x3), para track-cycling, para-table tennis and para-triathlon. Including compulsory and optional sports (disciplines), there shall be no more than 4 team sports on the programme of a Commonwealth Games. If basketball (3x3) is selected from the pool of optional sports, basketball (wheelchair para 3x3) becomes an obligatory event (or vice versa), in which case the sport of basketball shall be considered as one team sport. In cases when cricket is selected from the pool of optional sports, wheelchair basketball also becomes a part of the programme and can be an exception to the 4 team sports limit. Respecting local demands, an extra sport or some extra events can be included in this list, but they have to be approved by the Commonwealth Games Federation two years before that edition is held.[[144]](#cite_note-144) The current rules also determine gender parity, whereby men and women have an equal (or broadly equal) share of events.[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146)

### Input to list of disciplines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Input to list of disciplines")]

On 22 December 2017, the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") reported that the organisers of the games were in talks with the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC) about the inclusion of women's cricket.[[147]](#cite_note-147) In November 2018, the ICC confirmed that they have submitted a bid to include women's cricket in the Games. The bid was made in partnership with the [England and Wales Cricket Board](/wiki/England_and_Wales_Cricket_Board "England and Wales Cricket Board") (ECB).[[148]](#cite_note-148)

It was also reported that shooting was likely to be excluded from the games citing a lack of facilities around Birmingham. Shooting has been included at every Commonwealth Games since [Christchurch 1974](/wiki/Christchurch_1974 "Christchurch 1974"). In January 2018, the dropping of shooting from the games programme was confirmed by the then CEO of the CGF David Grevemberg.[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-:2-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151) In December 2018, The [International Shooting Sport Federation](/wiki/International_Shooting_Sport_Federation "International Shooting Sport Federation") (ISSF) delegation including President of ISSF [Vladimir Lisin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lisin "Vladimir Lisin") and CEO of [British Shooting](/wiki/British_Shooting "British Shooting") (BS) Hamish McInnes visited Birmingham and discussed with the Birmingham organising committee to add shooting in the 2022 Commonwealth Games.[[152]](#cite_note-152)

In December 2018, the [World Archery Federation](/wiki/World_Archery_Federation "World Archery Federation") (WA) confirmed that they had delivered a proposal for archery's inclusion in the Games. The bid was made in partnership with [Archery GB](/wiki/Archery_GB "Archery GB") and included [Aston Hall](/wiki/Aston_Hall "Aston Hall") as a suggested competition venue.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

In June 2019, after some time of popular searches and queries. Birmingham organising committee recommended the addition of two extra sports: para table tennis and beach volleyball. The proposal was approved by the CGF executive board.[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155)

The CGF officially announced on 13 August 2019 that the women's T20 cricket, beach volleyball and para table tennis have been included in the Games while due to infrastructure and logistics issues shooting had to be excluded and the proposal to add archery was rejected.[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160)

### Birmingham 2022 list of disciplines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Birmingham 2022 list of disciplines")]

With the changes in the Charter, and the review of options, the major changes from Gold Coast 2018 include the addition of judo to the core sports and the local organizers' optional decision to hold a women's cricket tournament, taking advantage of local infrastructure. Returning to the Games for the first time in [24 years](/wiki/1998_Commonwealth_Games "1998 Commonwealth Games"), due to various calendar issues, the cricket event was exclusively female for the first time.

First time events introduced at Birmingham 2022 are 3x3 basketball and its wheelchair counterpart. Birmingham will have the largest number of events available for women and athletes with disabilities in the history of the Games.[[161]](#cite_note-161)

On 27 October 2020, the list of events to be held in Birmingham was revealed with 271 finals scheduled for 19 sports. However, due to the low number of athletes registered in 15 events in 5 sports, they were in risk to be dropped and could not yet be confirmed on that exact time. However, with the exception of the 3 relays planned in Paralympic sports, all were confirmed in 2021, giving a total of 283.[[162]](#cite_note-162)[[163]](#cite_note-163) The final program was completed with 283 finals scheduled across 20 sports. This includes 136 events for women and 134 for men, an unprecedented gender balance amongst major multi-sport events though falling short of the [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games") when a perfect gender split had been achieved. In addition, 10 mixed gender events and 35 parasport events were held.

### Associated competitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Associated competitions")]

In January 2020, the [Indian Olympic Association](/wiki/Indian_Olympic_Association "Indian Olympic Association") (IOA), which functions also as the Commonwealth Games Association (CGA) representing India, submitted a proposal to the CGF to host a combined archery and shooting championships in [Chandigarh](/wiki/Chandigarh "Chandigarh") during January 2022. The proposal was endorsed by the [National Rifle Association of India](/wiki/National_Rifle_Association_of_India "National Rifle Association of India") (NRAI), the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India"), the ISSF and WA.[[164]](#cite_note-164)[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166) The CGF Executive Board approved the proposal at their meeting in [London](/wiki/London "London") which took place on 21–23 February 2020, and also confirmed that the 2022 Commonwealth Shooting and Archery Championships and the 2022 Commonwealth Games will be two separately organised and funded Commonwealth Sport events. The CGF shall issue a medal table one week following the Birmingham 2022 closing ceremony that includes results from Chandigarh 2022 as a further and final ranking of competing nations and territories from the respective competitions.[[167]](#cite_note-167)[[168]](#cite_note-168)[[169]](#cite_note-169) In July 2021, the CGF announced that the event had been cancelled due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in India](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_India "COVID-19 pandemic in India").[[170]](#cite_note-170)

In February 2022, the CGF announced that [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") would be included in the Games as a demonstration event and is in a possibility to be added at the games program at the future editions.[[171]](#cite_note-171) The inaugural [Commonwealth Esports Championships](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Esports_Championships "2022 Commonwealth Esports Championships") had separate branding, medals, and organisation and included both men and women's *[Dota 2](/wiki/Dota_2 "Dota 2")*, *[eFootball](/wiki/Pro_Evolution_Soccer "Pro Evolution Soccer")*, and *[Rocket League](/wiki/Rocket_League "Rocket League")* events.[[172]](#cite_note-172)

### Emblem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Emblem")]

The official emblem was unveiled on 27 July 2019 at [Centenary Square](/wiki/Centenary_Square "Centenary Square") during the Commonwealth Social festival. It was designed by local agency RBL, based in [Royal Leamington Spa](/wiki/Royal_Leamington_Spa "Royal Leamington Spa"), and is a jagged, triangular "B" shape formed by blue-yellow gradient lines representing the key venues of the Games in the West Midlands. The emblem is also the first to use the new branding for the CGF, as "Commonwealth Sport". It received a mainly positive reaction from locals in the city and on social media and some compared it to the emblem for the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") and [Paralympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Paralympics "2012 Summer Paralympics").[[176]](#cite_note-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177)

Morgan in the United Kingdom"), [Severn Trent](/wiki/Severn_Trent "Severn Trent") and [Reckitt](/wiki/Reckitt "Reckitt").[[178]](#cite_note-178)

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Mascot")]

Main article: [Perry (mascot)](/wiki/Perry_(mascot) "Perry (mascot)")

The official mascot for the Birmingham 2022 Commonwealth Games is [Perry](/wiki/Perry_(mascot) "Perry (mascot)"), a multi-coloured bull. Perry is named after an area of Birmingham, [Perry Barr](/wiki/Perry_Barr "Perry Barr"), within which Perry Park and the main athletics stadium, the [Alexander Stadium](/wiki/Alexander_Stadium "Alexander Stadium"), are located. The bull has a long history with being a symbol of Birmingham, and was positively received by the public on launch.[[179]](#cite_note-179) Perry was designed by ten-year-old Emma Lou from [Bolton](/wiki/Bolton "Bolton").[[180]](#cite_note-180) Perry was reproduced in coloured [willow](/wiki/Willow "Willow") in a Birmingham 2022 display commemorating the games at the [Chelsea Flower Show](/wiki/Chelsea_Flower_Show "Chelsea Flower Show") in 2022.[[181]](#cite_note-181)

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

In July 2020, production company [Sunset+Vine](/wiki/Sunset%2BVine "Sunset+Vine") was appointed as the host broadcaster for the event.[[182]](#cite_note-182) In October 2020, [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") acquired the United Kingdom rights.[[183]](#cite_note-:4-183) A record 57.1 million streams were generated on the BBC digital platforms by coverage of the 2022 Commonwealth Games, breaking the previous Commonwealth Games record. Additionally, the number of streams from the previous Commonwealth Games was more than six times higher than it was with this figure. The last event in [2018](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games") took place in Australia, which is in a completely different time zone. 28.6 million people watched 2022 Commonwealth Games on the BBC's linear channels in total. Prior to the women's 100-metre backstroke and men's 100-metre breaststroke finals, viewership peaked at 6.6 million viewers, while the [opening ceremony](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2022 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony") attracted a five-minute audience peak of 5.2 million. Audiences peaked at 5.1 million for the [women's 4 x 400-metre relay](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_4_%C3%97_400_metres_relay "Athletics at the 2022 Commonwealth Games – Women's 4 × 400 metres relay") final, while 4.8 million watched the closing ceremony. Other notable moments of the BBC's coverage included 4.2 million people watching the [men's 200-metre](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_200_metres "Athletics at the 2022 Commonwealth Games – Men's 200 metres") final and 4.1 million people watching [Adam Peaty](/wiki/Adam_Peaty "Adam Peaty") win the [50-metre breaststroke](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_50_metre_breaststroke "Swimming at the 2022 Commonwealth Games – Men's 50 metre breaststroke") final.[[184]](#cite_note-184)[[185]](#cite_note-185)
